coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of non-modified yellow emission silicon quantum dots and their visualization of hydrogen sulfide in living cells and onion tissues
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Na
Liang, Zhi-Yong
Fang, Wen-Le
Guo, Xiao-Feng
Wang, Hong
Zhang, Hui-Xian
coredata.dc:description: Yellow fluorescent silicon quantum dots (y-SiQDs) with 22.2% fluorescence quantum yield were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method using 3-glycidoxypropyl triethoxysilane (GOTS) and m-aminophenol. The excitation wavelength is 550 nm with an emission wavelength of 574 nm, which effectively avoids the interference of biological autofluorescence. Notably, the synthesis approach does not require any post-modification and the y-SiQDs can be directly used for hydrogen sulfide (H2S) quantification due to static quenching. It exhibits high sensitivity and excellent selectivity for H2S with a 0.2-10 μM (R2 = 0.9953) linear range and detection limit of 54 nM. y-SiQDs have excellent stability and biocompatibility and can be used for H2S imaging in living cells and onion tissues.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Hydrogen sulfide
Static quenching
Cell imaging
Onion tissues

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and H2S imaging of living cells
Material selection and formation of y-SiQDs
Exogenous and endogenous H2S imaging in living cells
Endogenous H2S imaging in onion tissue

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological analysis of y-SiQDs. (A) TEM image of y-SiQDs. The y-SiQDs are spherical particles. Scale bar: 100 nm; (b) Particle size distribution statistics of y-SiQDs. Calculating by 172 particles, the average particle size is 8.1 ± 1.2 nm.
Characterization of y-SiQDs. (A) XPS full scan of y-SiQDs. The surface of y-SiQDs contains four elements: Si, C, O and N; (B) FT-IR spectroscopy of y-SiQDs.

Fluorescence and UV-Vis properties of y-SiQDs. (A) The emission spectrum of y-SiQDs when the excitation wavelength is changed from 460 nm to 570 nm. (B) UV-Vis (blue line), fluorescence maximum excitation (black line) and maximum emission spectra (red line) of y-SiQDs in aqueous solution. The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths are 550/574 nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Changes in the fluorescence spectrum of y-SiQDs (50 μg mL−1) with the addition of 0-50 μM NaHS. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of y-SiQDs (50 μg mL−1) and the concentration of NaHS. NaHS concentration range: 0.2-10 μM. Excitation/emission wavelength: 550/574 nm. Error bars refer to the standard deviation, n = 3.

The change of F/F0 with adding different substances. K+, Na+, Ca+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, PO4 3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2 −, SO4 2 −, NO3 −, CO3 2 −, HCO3 −, Br−, I−, CH3COO−, GSH (Glutathione), Cys (Cysteine) and Hcy (Homocysteine): 500 μM. HSO3 −: 250 μM; NaHS: 50 μM. Excitation/emission wavelength: 550/574 nm. Error bars refer to the standard deviation, n = 3.
(A) Stern-Volmer equation at 15 °C and 37 °C. Excitation/emission wavelength: 550/574 nm. (B) Fluorescence lifetime of 50 μg mL−1 y-SiQDs before and after adding 15 μM NaHS.

Confocal fluorescence imaging of exogenous and endogenous H2S in HeLa cells. (A-C) HeLa cells only. (D-F) The yellow channel of HeLa cells incubated with 600 μg mL−1 y-SiQDs for 5 h. Excitation wavelength is 561 nm. (G-I) Fluorescence image of exogenous H2S in HeLa cells. The added NaHS concentration is 50 μM. (J-L) Fluorescence image of endogenous H2S in HeLa cells. Treated with 200 μM Cys and then incubated with 600 μg mL−1 y-SiQDs for 5 h. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence imaging of endogenous H2S in onion tissue. (A) (B) Onion tissue only. (C) (D) The yellow channel of onion tissue was incubated with 1.0 mg mL−1 of y-SiQDs for 12 h. (E) (F) After treatment with 5 mg mL−1 AOA for 1 h, the yellow channel of onion tissue was incubated with 1.0 mg mL−1 of y-SiQDs for 12 h. Excitation wavelength: 561 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of different fluorescent nanomaterials for the detection of H2S. “NM” means “not mentioned”.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of non-modified yellow emission silicon quantum dots and their visualization of hydrogen sulfide in living cells and onion tissues

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Agricultural Quality Standards and Testing Technology Research, Hubei Academy of Agricultural Sciences/Hubei Key Laboratory of Nutritional Quality and Safety of Agro Products, Wuhan 430064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Agricultural Quality Standards and Testing Technology Research
Hubei Academy of Agricultural Sciences/Hubei Key Laboratory of Nutritional Quality and Safety of Agro Products

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Agricultural Quality Standards and Testing Technology Research, Hubei Academy of Agricultural Sciences/Hubei Key Laboratory of Nutritional Quality and Safety of Agro Products, Wuhan 430064, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of y-SiQDs and their application to image H2S in cells and onion tissue.

Yellow fluorescent silicon quantum dots (y-SiQDs) with 22.2% fluorescence quantum yield were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method using 3-glycidoxypropyl triethoxysilane (GOTS) and m-aminophenol. The excitation wavelength is 550 nm with an emission wavelength of 574 nm, which effectively avoids the interference of biological autofluorescence. Notably, the synthesis approach does not require any post-modification and the y-SiQDs can be directly used for hydrogen sulfide (H2S) quantification due to static quenching. It exhibits high sensitivity and excellent selectivity for H2S with a 0.2-10 μM (R2 = 0.9953) linear range and detection limit of 54 nM. y-SiQDs have excellent stability and biocompatibility and can be used for H2S imaging in living cells and onion tissues.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is considered to be an endogenous gasotransmitter discovered after NO and CO [1]. Endogenous H2S production is associated with various enzymes [2] and is involved in the regulation of important physiological processes, such as the regulation of the nervous, immune, and endocrine systems and the control of inflammation [3–4]. For example, H2S protects neurons from death by increasing reduced glutathione levels, acts as a vasodilator in the mammalian vasculature [5], up-regulates the expression of histone deacetylase 3 and inhibits the level of histone acetylation then reduces the transcription of proinflammatory factors [6], ect. Previously, H2S was considered a hazardous gas and the dangerous concentration is 100 ppm in the air [7]. In healthy animals and humans, physiological concentrations of circulating H2S are estimated to range from 10 to 100 μM, the toxic levels of H2S are less than two times higher than its endogenous level in rat brain tissue [8]. Abnormal levels of H2S are related to many diseases, including Alzheimer's disease, Down's syndrome, diabetes and hypertension, etc [9–10]. Until now, the regulatory functions of H2S are not fully understood due to the difficulty in accurately quantifying H2S concentrations in biological systems [11].

Fluorescence technique is an excellent strategy for tracking H2S and studying its biological functions due to its temporal and spatial resolution. To date, fluorescent nanomaterials like metal-organic frameworks [12–14], semiconductor quantum dots [15–16], metal nanoclusters [17–19] and carbon dots [5,20] have been reported for the quantification of H2S. Most of the reports on the detection of H2S are mainly based on two ways: introducing reactive groups into the material, or combining H2S with metal ions to form metal sulfide precipitates (such as CuS). For example, Wang et al. [21] used surface modification of CdTe quantum dots with azidocoumarin-4-acetic acid to detect H2S, in which the azide group is the response group. Biswas et al. [22] developed a method to detect H2S using 2-azido-1,4-phthalic acid as one of the ligands in the synthesis of Al-MIL-53-N3 MOF. In this method, H2S reduces the azide group to form amine. Yang's group [23] constructed a dual-emission nanoprobe by combining CuNCs and carbon dots. In the presence of H2S, the CuNCs react with H2S to form CuS, which quenches the fluorescence of CuNCs. The fluorescence of the carbon dots remained unchanged, resulting in the creation of H2S ratio fluorescent nanoprobe. Zhang and coworkers [24] designed an “on-off-on” sensing platform that can be used to detect H2S. The fluorescence quenching of PEI-CDs was induced by Cu2+, but the strong interaction between Cu and S restores the fluorescence of the Cu-PEI-CDs composite system after the addition of H2S. It should be noted that the mentioned surface modification strategies involve multi-step reactions, which limits their practical application. Moreover, heavy metals contained in fluorescent nanomaterials or composite systems may be toxic to biological systems.

Research interest in fluorescent silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) is growing in recent years [25–26]. Due to their high natural abundance, unique optical properties, good water solubility and non-toxicity, SiQDs have been widely applied for biosensing and long-term in vivo / in vitro bioimaging [27–30]. Up to now, two works have been reported on SiQDs-related fluorescent probes for the detection of H2S. Zhang's group [31] prepared SiNPs with an emission wavelength of 450 nm. After interacting with Cr (VI), the fluorescence is quenched to form SiNPs-Cr (VI) composite, and then the blue fluorescence could be restored by the addition of H2S, with LOD of 22 nM. Fu et al. [32] proposed a surface modification strategy to realize H2S detection. Azide-linked 1,8-naphthalimide fluorophore was modified on the surface of SiNDs. The azide group served as the reaction site for H2S, and the fluorescence change of 1,8-naphthalimide at 550 nm was used to detect H2S. The SiNDs effectively improve the water solubility of the probe. The linear range and LOD are 10-100 μM and 0.2 μM, respectively. In addition, these two works employed the strategy of composite systems or surface modification. As far as we know, a one-step approach for the direct determination of H2S based on long-wavelength and non-modified SiQDs has not been reported.

Taking into account the above facts, SiQDs with yellow fluorescence (y-SiQDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using 3-glycidoxypropyl triethoxysilane (GOTS) and m-aminophenol. The emission wavelength reaches 574 nm, which is longer than that of most SiQDs prepared by the same method. H2S quenched the fluorescence of non-modified y-SiQDs via static quenching that enabled the direct detection of H2S with a detection limit of 54 nM. The method is highly selective, and biological ions and some small molecules do not affect the detection of H2S. Moreover, y-SiQDs have been further used for imaging of H2S in HeLa cells and onion tissue.
Reagents and instruments are shown in supplementary materials.

Firstly, 1.5 mL of GOTS was added to a flask containing 5.0 mL of ultrapure water, and 5.0 mL of anhydrous ethanol was added to promote dissolution of GOTS with continuous stirring. Then, 50.0 mg of m-aminophenol was added to the above solution and stirred until the m-aminophenol was completely dissolved. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. The resulting dark purple solution was extracted with dichloromethane and water, and the supernatant was taken and repeated several times. The supernatant was collected and then dried in a vacuum freezer to obtain purple solids as y-SiQDs. The resulting y-SiQDs were formulated into a 5 mg mL− 1 stock solution and stored in a 4 °C freezer for further use.

Using cresyl violet dissolved in methanol as the standard (QY = 54%) [33], the fluorescence quantum yield of y-SiQDs was calculated according to the following formula [34]:
∅ =
∅ s
I
A s
η
2
/
I s
A
η
s
2

∅ refers to the quantum yield of y-SiQDs. A and I are absorbance and integrated fluorescence intensity, respectively. The absorbance of each substance in the experiment is less than 0.05. η indicates the refractive index of the solvent (the refractive indices of methanol and water are 1.329 and 1.333, respectively). Subscript s represents the standard cresyl violet.

30 μL of y-SiQDs (5.0 mg mL−1) was added to a volume of phosphate buffer (PBS, 20 mM, pH = 7.40). After adding different concentrations of NaHS solution, the total solution volume was 3.0 mL. After incubation at 37 ℃ for 4 min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The excitation wavelength was 550 nm, and the excitation and emission slits were both set to 5 nm. The experiment was carried out three times in parallel.
Details are available in supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and H2S imaging of living cells
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM containing 1% penicillin-streptomycin and seeded in dishes at 37 °C for 24 h. For H2S imaging, HeLa cells were incubated with 600 μg mL−1 y-SiQDs for 5 h. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove y-SiQDs, and then further incubated with NaHS (50 μM) for 30 min. For endogenous H2S imaging, cells were incubated with 200 μM Cys (the main precursor for H2S production by enzymatic catalysis) [32] for 1 h before incubation with y-SiQDs. Immediately after washing off the solution with PBS, fluorescence imaging was performed under a confocal microscope.

Endogenous H2S imaging of onion tissue was conducted. Onion inner epidermal tissues were incubated with 1.0 mg mL−1 y-SiQDs in PBS (10 mM, pH 7.40) for 12 h in the dark. For endogenous H2S imaging, the onion tissues were placed in a petri dish and incubated with 5 mM carboxymethoxyamine hemihydrochloride (AOA, H2S inhibitor) for 1 h followed by y-SiQDs. Onion tissue was washed three times with PBS before fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material selection and formation of y-SiQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Organosilanes are commonly used as precursors for the synthesis of SiQDs. For example, N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO) [35], 3-(aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS) [36], 1-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride [37] and 3-glycidoxypropyl trimethoxysilane (GPTMS) [38] have been used in the synthesis of SiQDs. Using DAMO as the silicon source and o-aminophenol as the reducing agent, Han et al. synthesized long-wavelength SiQDs with an emission of 550 nm [39]. In this work, we investigated the properties of SiQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method with different silicon sources with o-, m-, and p-aminophenol as reducing agents, respectively, hoping to obtain long-wavelength emission SiQDs. Expect for the reported DAMO, we selected 3-glycidoxypropyl triethoxysilane (GOTS) and unreported silicon sources including 3-mercaptopropyl triethoxysilane (MPTES) and 3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl urea (TOSPU). The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of the obtained SiQDs are listed in Table S1. It was found that when GOTS and m-aminophenol were used as raw materials, the emission wavelength of the obtained SiQDs was the longest, reaching 574 nm. The possible formation mechanism of SiQDs has been hypothesized to involve the hydrolysis of GOTS to 3-glycidyloxypropyl trihydroxysilane. Then, in the presence of the reducing agent m-aminophenol, the three hydroxyl groups are spontaneously reduced, resulting in the growth of y-SiQDs [40]. It was also found that among common ions and small molecules, only H2S could quench the fluorescence of the SiQDs. After that, a series of experiments were carried out.

The characterization of y-SiQDs was confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray powder diffraction pattern (XRD). It can be observed from the TEM images that the y-SiQDs are spherical particles (Fig. 1 A). By calculating the particle size of 172 particles, the average particle size is obtained to be 8.1 ± 1.2 nm. The size distribution histogram of y-SiQDs is shown in Fig. 1B. The elemental composition of y-SiQDs was obtained by XPS analysis. Fig. 2 A shows that four elements are contained including C, N, O, and Si in y-SiQDs. The peaks at 102.4 eV, 286.4 eV, 403.4 eV and 536.4 eV are corresponded to Si 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution patterns of C 1s, O 1s and Si 2p are shown in the supplementary materials (Fig. S1 A-C). To obtain the surface functional groups of y-SiQDs, FT-TR characterization was performed (Fig. 2B). The absorption peak of 3404 cm−1 indicates that the -OH and -NH2 are contained on the surface of y-SiQDs. The -CH2 is confirmed by absorption peaks at 2935 cm−1, 2827 cm−1 and 1458 cm−1. The peak at 1623 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of -NH2. The absorption peaks at 1199 cm−1 and 1112 cm−1 correspond to C–O–C/Si-O-Si bonds, and the peak at 916 cm−1 represents Si-O bonds. The peak for ethylene oxide groups did not appear. A possible reason is that it disappeared during the synthesis process. The surface composition of y-SiQDs by XPS is consistent with the FT-IR results.

The optical properties of y-SiQDs are explored by fluorescence spectra and UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when the excitation wavelength was 460-570 nm, the fluorescence intensity increased, but the position of the fluorescence peak hardly changed. This suggests that y-SiQDs are excitation independent. Fig. 3B shows that the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of y-SiQDs are 550/574 nm, respectively. The two absorption peaks of y-SiQDs are at 352 nm and 553 nm, respectively. We chose several water-miscible solvents, such as N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), etc., to evaluate the properties of water-soluble y-SiQDs. Fig. S2 shows the fluorescence emission spectroscopy of y-SiQDs measured at an excitation wavelength of 550 nm in water/organic solvent 1:1 (v/v) medium. The results indicate that the emission peak position of y-SiQDs changes slightly under the excitation of 550 nm. Although the fluorescence intensity in water:solvent 1:1 (v/v) is stronger than in aqueous solution, we performed the experiments in aqueous medium considering the toxicity of organic solvents and subsequent biological imaging. Cresyl violet was chosen as the reference, and the QY of y-SiQDs was calculated as 22.2%. The pH stability, photostability, physiological conditions stability, temperature stability and ionic strength stability of y-SiQDs were also investigated, the results are given in supplementary materials (Fig. S3).

Before carrying on H2S detection, we optimized the detection conditions involving pH and reaction time. Due to the need for in vivo imaging, experiments were performed at 37 °C. The experimental results are provided in the supplementary materials (Fig. S4). The detection of H2S can be conducted under the condition of pH 7.40, and the reaction time is 4 min. The fluorescence intensity of y-SiQDs decreased continuously as the NaHS concentration increased from 0 to 50 μM (Fig. 4 A), and the fluorescence intensity displays a good linear relationship with the concentration of NaHS in the range of 0.2-10 μM (R2 = 0.9953). The LOD is calculated as 54 nM (signal-to-noise is 3). The comparison of y-SiQDs with other previously reported fluorescent nanomaterials for H2S detection is listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that the y-SiQDs have long wavelength and the LOD is comparable to these reports. This facilitates the avoidance of interference from biological autofluorescence (blue fluorescence) during imaging, which can improve the accuracy of the experiment to some extent. In addition, SiQDs are usually non-toxic, which can effectively avoid the potential toxicity problems of metal-based quantum dots or noble-metal nanoclusters, making them ideal probes for bioimaging.

The selectivity of y-SiQDs was investigated. Among metal ions, anions and other sulfur-containing species in organisms (SO4 2 −, HSO3 − and thiols), only H2S can cause fluorescence quenching of y-SiQDs (Fig. 5 ). The concentration of HSO3 − is 5 times higher than that of H2S, and the other substances are 10 times that of H2S. We tested the effect of several amino acids, proteins [42–43] and NO on the fluorescence of y-SiQDs in selective experiments. In addition, we continuously introduce CO, CO2 and O2 gas into the y-SiQDs solution. These substances on the fluorescence of y-SiQDs were negligible (Fig. S5). The above results show that the established method has high selectivity for the detection of H2S.

To validate the reliability of the proposed method, we employed it to detect H2S in actual samples. Specifically, we investigated onion, broccoli and beer in this study. Onion and broccoli were prepared underwent the following procedure [44]: 1.0 g of each sample was soaked overnight in 0.2% NaOH (m/m), boiled until dissolved, centrifuged at 5000 r/min for 5 min, and the supernatant was adjusted to neutral with 1 M HCl. The beer samples were adjusted to neutral with 1 M HCl. The results are shown in Table S2, which indicate that our method could sensitively detect H2S in these samples with recoveries of 95.8%-103.8% and RSDs between 1.1% and 6.3%. These outcomes demonstrate that the developed method had good stability and reliability to detect H2S in actual samples.

By studying the temperature dependence of fluorescence quenching as well as the fluorescence lifetime, we can distinguish whether H2S-induced fluorescence quenching is dynamic quenching or static quenching [45]. According to the Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F
=
1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where Ksv and [Q] are the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and the concentration of H2S, respectively. The quenching constants at 15 °C and 37 °C were investigated. It can be seen from Fig. 6 A that the slope of the linearity, that is, Ksv decreases with increasing temperature, indicating that it is caused by static quenching [46]. This is because in static quenching, the quencher H2S binds to the y-SiQDs in the ground state to form non-fluorescent or relatively weak fluorescent complexes. At higher temperatures, the stability of the complex decreases hence the quenching constant becomes smaller [46]. In addition, fluorescence lifetimes before and after the reaction were measured (Fig. 6B). The lifetime of y-SiQDs is 3.40 ± 0.06 ns and 3. 29 ± 0.04 after reaction. The fluorescence lifetime did not change significantly, revealing the existence of static quenching [41]. This is because non-fluorescent or weakly fluorescent complexes are formed in the ground state, so the unbound fluorophores show their intrinsic lifetime [45]. According to the above results, it is proved that H2S causes the fluorescence quenching of y-SiQDs through static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exogenous and endogenous H2S imaging in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of y-SiQDs on HeLa cells was investigated by MTT assay before cell imaging. After 24 h of incubation with 0-1100 μg mL−1 of y-SiQDs, the cells still maintained good viability (Fig. S6). At the experimental concentration of 600 μg mL−1, the cell viability was over 87%. These studies demonstrate that the experimental concentrations of y-SiQDs used for cellular imaging are virtually non-toxic to cells.

Exogenous and endogenous H2S imaging in HeLa cells was investigated to evaluate the biological applications of y-SiQDs. After incubation with 600 μg mL−1 of y-SiQDs for 5 h, bright yellow fluorescence appeared in HeLa cells (Fig. 7 D-F). For the imaging of exogenous H2S, 50 μM of NaHS was added for further incubation for 30 min, and the yellow fluorescence in cells was significantly weakened (Fig. 7 G-I). For endogenous H2S imaging, incubate with 200 μM Cys for 1 h prior to incubation with y-SiQDs. As shown in Fig. 7 J-L, 200 μM Cys can effectively generate H2S, thereby weakening the yellow fluorescence in HeLa cells. These imaging results reveal that y-SiQDs have the ability to conduct fluorescence imaging of endogenous H2S in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Endogenous H2S imaging in onion tissue
ce:section.ce:para: H2S is thought to play an important regulatory role in plant tissues, regulating and influencing many signaling events and cellular physiological processes [47]. The onion tissue was chosen as the experimental object for endogenous H2S fluorescence imaging. As presented in Fig. 8 , weak fluorescence was observed in onion tissue compared to the dark background of unincubated y-SiQDs. Onion tissue showed bright yellow fluorescence after incubation with 5 mM AOA (H2S inhibitor) for 1 h before treatment with y-SiQDs for 12 h. Based on these results, we speculated that H2S was produced in onion tissue.

In conclusion, long-wavelength emission y-SiQDs can be synthesized by 3-glycidoxypropyl triethoxysilane (GOTS) and m-aminophenol as precursors via a one-step method. H2S can directly quench the fluorescence of y-SiQDs by static quenching. The detection of H2S by y-SiQDs shows high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. Furthermore, the excellent stability and potency of y-SiQDs are sufficient to enable imaging of exogenous and endogenous H2S in HeLa cells and endogenous H2S in onion tissue. Therefore, this method is a milestone for long-wavelength SiQDs for H2S detection, which provides a biocompatible nano platform. The desirable properties of the y-SiQDs can be used in various applications, especially in the imaging and medical analysis of animals as well as plants.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

Na Wei: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization. Zhi-Yong Liang: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization. Wen-Le Fang: Investigation, Visualization. Xiao-Feng Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Hong Wang: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Hui-Xian Zhang: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32070398, Beijing, China) and Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2022CFB232, Hubei, China).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.03.143.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
